**Boone** ([/ˈbuːn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*BOON*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a city in [Des Moines Township](/wiki/Des_Moines_Township,_Boone_County,_Iowa "Des Moines Township, Boone County, Iowa") and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Boone County, Iowa](/wiki/Boone_County,_Iowa "Boone County, Iowa"), United States.[[3]](#cite_note-GR6-3)

It is the principal city of the Boone, Iowa [Micropolitan Statistical Area](/wiki/Micropolitan_Statistical_Area "Micropolitan Statistical Area"), which encompasses all of Boone County. This micropolitan statistical area, along with the [Ames, Iowa](/wiki/Ames,_Iowa "Ames, Iowa") [Metropolitan Statistical Area](/wiki/Metropolitan_Statistical_Area "Metropolitan Statistical Area") comprise the larger Ames-Boone, Iowa [Combined Statistical Area](/wiki/Combined_Statistical_Area "Combined Statistical Area"). The population of the city was reported as 12,460 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").[[4]](#cite_note-cen2020-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boone,_Iowa&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/BooneMines.png/250px-BooneMines.png)](/wiki/File:BooneMines.png)

Map of the Boone area from 1908, showing the railroads and coal mines (shown in red) of the region.

Coal mining played an important part in the early history of the Boone area. Local blacksmiths were already mining coal from the banks of Honey Creek south of what became Boone in 1849.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Boone was [platted](/wiki/Plat "Plat") as a town in 1865 by [John Insley Blair](/wiki/John_Insley_Blair "John Insley Blair"). It was incorporated the following year, when the [Chicago and North Western Transportation Company](/wiki/Chicago_and_North_Western_Transportation_Company "Chicago and North Western Transportation Company") railroad station was built there. The town was originally named "Montana";[[6]](#cite_note-6) it was renamed to Boone in 1871.[[7]](#cite_note-boonecounty-7) The nearby town of Boonesboro was also chartered in 1866; Boonesboro was annexed to Boone in 1887.[[7]](#cite_note-boonecounty-7) The present name is a tribute to [Nathan Boone](/wiki/Nathan_Boone "Nathan Boone"), son of explorer [Daniel Boone](/wiki/Daniel_Boone "Daniel Boone").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Commercial mining was spurred by the arrival of the railroad. In 1867, Canfield and Taylor opened a mine just west of town. Their [mine shaft](/wiki/Shaft_mining "Shaft mining") was 242 feet deep, and they hauled coal to the railroad by wagon. In 1874, a [railroad spur](/wiki/Spur_line "Spur line") was built to the mine. This mine was taken over by the railroad, and operated for 30 years.
There were [coal seams](/wiki/Coal_seam "Coal seam") in the Boone area; the upper vein, about 3 feet thick, was always worked using [longwall mining](/wiki/Longwall_mining "Longwall mining"), while the lower vein was always mined using [room and pillar](/wiki/Room_and_pillar "Room and pillar") mining.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In 1912, [United Mine Workers](/wiki/United_Mine_Workers "United Mine Workers") Local 869 in Boone had 554 members, close to 10% of the population at the time.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The grocery chain [Fareway](/wiki/Fareway "Fareway") originated in Boone. The company maintains their business offices in town also.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/First_Casey%27s_store_in_Boone%2C_IA.jpg/250px-First_Casey%27s_store_in_Boone%2C_IA.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_Casey%27s_store_in_Boone,_IA.jpg)

Original Casey's store in Boone, IA as it looked in March 2022

The first [Casey's General Store](/wiki/Casey%27s_General_Stores "Casey's General Stores") was founded in Boone, Iowa in 1968.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Other larger businesses that have had or still maintain facilities in Boone include Gates Rubber, Heinrich Envelope, Archway Bakery, Lowe-Berry Seeds, Coca-Cola Bottling, Quinn Iron & Wire Works (now Besser Quinn Machine & Foundry), Mid-States Steel, Sunoco Products, Specialty Leather Productions, Randy's Frozen Meats, PDM Distribution, ProLiant Biologicals, APC, Thermomass Composite Tech, CDS Global, Patterson Logistics and Stoll Bottling.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boone,_Iowa&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 9.02 square miles (23.36 km2), all land.[[14]](#cite_note-Gazetteer_files-14)

[Ledges State Park](/wiki/Ledges_State_Park "Ledges State Park") is located four miles south of Boone and is a popular destination.

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boone,_Iowa&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

At the 2000 census,[[18]](#cite_note-GR2-18) there were 12,803 people, 5,313 households and 3,363 families living in the city. The population density was 1,433.9 inhabitants per square mile (553.6/km2). There were 5,585 housing units at an average density of 625.5 units per square mile (241.5 units/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 98.34% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 0.32% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 0.22% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 0.21% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.35% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 0.56% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race were 0.87% of the population.

There were 5,313 households, of which 30.2% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 51.0% were married couples living together, 9.8% had a female householder with no husband present, and 36.7% were non-families. 31.5% of all households were made up of individuals, and 13.8% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.34 and the average family size was 2.94.

24.3% of the population were under the age of 18, 9.5% from 18 to 24, 27.2% from 25 to 44, 21.4% from 45 to 64, and 17.6% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 38 years. For every 100 females, there were 89.3 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 85.4 males.

The [median household income](/wiki/Median_household_income "Median household income") was $43,256. Males had a median income of $32,106 and females $22,119. The per capita income was $22,611. About 5.4% of families and 8.4% of the population were below the poverty line, including 10.9% of those under age 18 and 5.8% of those age 65 or over.

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boone,_Iowa&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

At the 2010 [census](/wiki/Census "Census"),[[19]](#cite_note-wwwcensusgov-19) there were 12,661 people, 5,380 households and 3,278 families living in the city. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 1,403.7 inhabitants per square mile (542.0/km2). There were 5,917 housing units at an average density of 656.0 units per square mile (253.3 units/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 96.7% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 0.8% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 0.3% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 0.4% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.5% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), and 1.3% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race were 2.0% of the population.

There were 5,380 households, of which 29.4% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 47.3% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 9.7% had a female householder with no husband present, 3.9% had a male householder with no wife present, and 39.1% were non-families. 32.1% of all households were made up of individuals, and 12.5% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.32 and the average family size was 2.92.

The median age was 38.1 years. 23.5% of residents were under the age of 18; 9.3% were between the ages of 18 and 24; 25% were from 25 to 44; 26.1% were from 45 to 64; and 16.2% were 65 years of age or older. The gender makeup of the city was 48.9% male and 51.1% female.

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boone,_Iowa&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [census](/wiki/United_States_census "United States census") of 2020,[[20]](#cite_note-20) there were 12,460 people, 5,410 households, and 3,228 families residing in the city. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 1,371.6 inhabitants per square mile (529.6/km2). There were 5,935 housing units at an average density of 653.3 per square mile (252.2/km2). The [racial](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States_census "Race and ethnicity in the United States census") makeup of the city was 92.8% [White](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans"), 1.2% [Black or African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 0.3% [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), 0.6% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), 0.0% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_Americans "Pacific Islander Americans"), 0.9% from other races and 4.2% from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") persons of any race comprised 2.6% of the population.

Of the 5,410 households, 26.9% of which had children under the age of 18 living with them, 44.8% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 7.1% were cohabitating couples, 27.9% had a female householder with no spouse or partner present and 20.2% had a male householder with no spouse or partner present. 40.3% of all households were non-families. 34.1% of all households were made up of individuals, 14.6% had someone living alone who was 65 years old or older.

The median age in the city was 41.2 years. 24.0% of the residents were under the age of 20; 5.1% were between the ages of 20 and 24; 25.4% were from 25 and 44; 25.3% were from 45 and 64; and 20.2% were 65 years of age or older. The gender makeup of the city was 48.6% male and 51.4% female.

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boone,_Iowa&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Boone is located on [U.S. Route 30](/wiki/U.S._Route_30_in_Iowa "U.S. Route 30 in Iowa"). The original [Lincoln Highway](/wiki/Lincoln_Highway "Lincoln Highway") ran through the center of town, but a new four-lane highway was built in the late 1960s that bypassed the center of Boone to the south. There is also a network of paved country roads radiating out in all directions.

Boone is served by the mainline of the [Union Pacific](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad"), which purchased the [Chicago & North Western Railroad](/wiki/Chicago_%26_North_Western_Railroad "Chicago & North Western Railroad") (C&NW) in 1995. Boone was a division and crew change point on the railroad under the Chicago & North Western and some of that business remains today. There is a moderate-sized rail yard to the east of the downtown area. The locomotives no longer sound their horns in the town proper due to modifications to the various points where streets intersect with the rail line.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Boone was also the exact midpoint on the Fort Dodge, Des Moines & Southern Railroad, an electric interurban line converted to diesel in 1955 after a flood on the Des Moines River devastated the [Fraser](/wiki/Fraser,_Iowa "Fraser, Iowa") hydroelectric power plant that supplied power to the railroad. It connected with the C&NW downtown. It was purchased by the C&NW in 1968 and subsequently abandoned. The western part of the line to Fraser has been resurrected as the [Boone and Scenic Valley Railroad](/wiki/Boone_and_Scenic_Valley_Railroad "Boone and Scenic Valley Railroad"), a tourist line.[[22]](#cite_note-History-22)

The [Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Chicago,_Milwaukee,_St._Paul_and_Pacific_Railroad "Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad") also ran a branch line into Boone from the southeast. It likewise connected with the C&NW. The line was trimmed back to [Luther](/wiki/Luther,_Iowa "Luther, Iowa") in the late 1960s and completely abandoned a few years later.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boone,_Iowa&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Education")]

The [Boone Community School District](/wiki/Boone_Community_School_District "Boone Community School District"), which operates public schools, covers almost all of the Boone city limits. [Boone High School](/wiki/Boone_High_School "Boone High School") is the comprehensive high school of that district. A small piece of the city limits is within the [United Community School District](/wiki/United_Community_School_District "United Community School District"),[[24]](#cite_note-24) which operates an elementary school. United CSD sends secondary students to Boone CSD and [Ames CSD](/wiki/Ames_Community_School_District "Ames Community School District").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

The Sacred Heart parish operates a parochial school for kindergarten through 8th grade. Trinity Lutheran Church operates a school for students 3 years old to 8th grade.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The [Des Moines Area Community College](/wiki/Des_Moines_Area_Community_College "Des Moines Area Community College") (DMACC) has a campus in the south central part of the town. It was established when DMACC purchased the former Boone Junior College in 1969 and was the second campus added during the initial period of expansion.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boone,_Iowa&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

### Festivals and events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boone,_Iowa&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Festivals and events")]

Pufferbilly Days, an annual event celebrating Boone's railroad heritage, is held at the end of the summer and is one of Iowa's top five community festivals with over 30,000 attendees.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The IMCA Super Nationals are held at the Boone Speedway, occurring during the week of Labor Day in September.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Boone is also a site of the annual [Farm Progress Show](/wiki/Farm_Progress_Show "Farm Progress Show"). Originally the show rotated from one farm to another in Iowa, Illinois and Indiana before the permanent sites were established at Boone, Iowa and [Decatur, Illinois](/wiki/Decatur,_Illinois "Decatur, Illinois"). The show now alternates between these two permanent sites on an annual basis.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Tourist attractions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Boone,_Iowa&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Tourist attractions")]

[Vincenzo Miserendino](/wiki/Vincenzo_Miserendino "Vincenzo Miserendino")'s final and largest statue of [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"), which was eight times life-size was commissioned in 1941, and was scheduled to arrive in Boone, Iowa, in 1943. However, before casting, its 4,000 pounds of bronze was appropriated for World War II. Miserendino commented that Theodore Roosevelt himself would have approved of that decision to put the service of the country first. Later, in 1946, the Roman Bronze Corporation of [Corona, New York City](/wiki/Corona,_Queens "Corona, Queens"), produced the casting in Miserendino's original mold.[[a]](#cite_note-31) On January 6, 1948, it was placed in [McHose Park](/w/index.php?title=McHose_Park&action=edit&redlink=1 "McHose Park (page does not exist)") [[ceb](https://ceb.wikipedia.org/wiki/McHose_Park "ceb:McHose Park")].[[b]](#cite_note-32) Residing on three large granite blocks, the 6' 3" statue depicts Roosevelt rising out of a mountainous landscape with his hand outstretched as if addressing a crowd. Below the statue are ten bas reliefs of Roosevelt's outdoor accomplishments.[[31]](#cite_note-33)

[Boone & Scenic Valley Railroad](/wiki/Boone_%26_Scenic_Valley_Railroad "Boone & Scenic Valley Railroad") & the James H. Andrew Railroad Museum are other points of interest. The B&SVRR allows visitors to view Boone and the Des Moines river valley area via train, riding behind various historic train equipment. Thomas the Tank Engine makes annual visits to B&SVRR, at the Day Out with Thomas event, usually in September.[[32]](#cite_note-34) The railroad also hosts several "dinner" trains, a regular weekly train excursion as well as a special Christmas themed event. The James H. Andrew museum, located on site, highlights railroad history with a variety of exhibits and authentic railroad related artifacts.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

[Boone County Historical Center](/wiki/Boone_County_Historical_Center "Boone County Historical Center"), a museum housed in the former Champlin Memorial Masonic Temple.[[34]](#cite_note-36)[[35]](#cite_note-37)

On July 25, 1924, McHose and his wife, Ella, gave nearly 200 acres of land on the southwest side of Boone to the city for a park which is named in their honor. When McHose died in June of 1927 at the age of 78, shortly after the park's dedication, his last will and testament specified that a statue of Roosevelt be placed in the park. John W.
